sections.heading: Core Concepts
sections.content: Bubble Sort is a foundational **sorting algorithm** that repeatedly compares and **swaps** [[primary|adjacent elements]]. Like bubbles in water, each round gradually floats the unsorted extreme value toward the boundary of the sequence.
items: While **traversing** the sequence, only one pair of [[primary|adjacent elements]] is compared at a time; if they are out of order, a **swap** is performed
After each full **iteration**, the maximum of the unsorted portion bubbles up to its [[primary|correct position]] at the end
The steps repeat until a round completes without any **swap**, indicating the sequence has reached [[primary|full sorted order]]
Because swaps occur only on **strict greater-than**, equal elements never trade places — making it a [[success|stable sort]]

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: Bubble Sort's runtime depends heavily on the initial state of the data and degrades in the worst case. However, it is an in-place algorithm with minimal memory overhead and excellent space efficiency.
items: **Best-case time complexity**: [[warning|O(n)]] — with early termination, an already sorted array requires only one pass and no swaps
**Worst-case and average time complexity**: [[danger|O(n²)]] — reversed or random data forces the nested loops through many comparisons and swaps
**Space complexity**: [[success|O(1)]] — sorting happens in place on the original array, using only a constant amount of extra memory

sections.heading: What It Optimizes
sections.content: Bubble Sort is not suited to large, random datasets — **insertion sort** is usually a better choice. But in memory-constrained environments or when the data is **nearly sorted**, it still shines with early termination.
items: **Early termination**: if a round completes with no swaps, the array is already **fully sorted** and the loop can break, saving time
**Memory-constrained environments**: with a **space complexity** of [[success|O(1)]], it remains valuable on hardware like microcontrollers with very limited memory
**Nearly sorted data**: when only a few elements are out of place, it can finish in nearly [[warning|O(n)]], making it ideal for fine-tuning data
**Bidirectional traversal optimization**: switch to **cocktail sort**, which alternates direction to push extremes toward both boundaries faster

sections.heading: When to Use It
sections.content: Though unsuitable for large datasets, Bubble Sort remains a practical, intuitive choice in memory-constrained environments, with nearly sorted data, or when a quick hand-written implementation is required, thanks to its minimal logic and [[success|O(1)]] space advantage.
items: **Microcontroller environments**: when memory is extremely limited and only tiny arrays need to be processed, the [[success|O(1)]] space complexity is crucial
**Nearly sorted streams**: for example, occasionally out-of-order IoT timestamps — combined with early termination, performance approaches [[warning|O(n)]]
**No-library hand-coding**: in exams or extreme environments without built-in tools, it is the simplest algorithm to write from scratch

sections.heading: Nested Loop Boundaries
sections.content: Beginners are most often tripped up by the **nested loop logic** — an outer loop that controls the **number of passes** and an inner loop whose comparison range gradually shrinks. Understanding [[primary|boundary setup]] is the key to writing a correct implementation.
items: The outer loop controls the **number of passes**; since each round fixes one extreme in place, at most `n-1` passes are needed
The inner loop compares **adjacent elements**, and its boundary (e.g., `n-i-1`) must shrink each pass to avoid re-comparing the sorted tail
Each comparison accesses indices `j` and `j+1`; if the inner offset is not subtracted correctly, it easily triggers an [[danger|array out-of-bounds]] error

sections.heading: Common Variants
sections.content: Beginners often get confused because online tutorials show code going in different directions. The two main variants are "**push the maximum to the right**" and "**float the minimum to the left**".
items: **Push max rightward**: the common [[primary|Upward]] variant — traverse from the left and push the unsorted maximum to the right end
**Float min leftward**: the original [[primary|Backward]] variant — traverse from the right and let smaller elements bubble toward the left
**Identical performance**: the two share the exact same **time complexity**; only the traversal direction and logic differ, so understanding both prevents implementation confusion

sections.heading: Fun Facts
sections.content: Beyond the algorithm itself, Bubble Sort hides a few delightful trivia: an exact swap-count formula, Python's elegant [[primary|one-line swap]] idiom, and why modern languages have all but retired it.
items: **Worst-case swap count**: for a fully reversed array, the swaps are exactly `n(n-1)/2` — a length-5 reversed array needs [[warning|10]] swaps
**Pythonic swap**: with `a, b = b, a` multiple assignment, two values trade places in one line — no `temp` variable required
**Long retired in practice**: Python's built-in [[primary|Timsort]] and Java's **Dual-Pivot Quicksort** both run in [[success|O(n log n)]], so Bubble Sort survives mainly as a teaching tool
